name: Brandied Fruit Cakes (Sharon)
recipeInstructions:
NOTE I take the total amount of fruit (10 cups) and make a nice-looking mixture. I don't necessarily use the exact amounts shown. If the candied fruit is on sale, I just use more of it than the raisins. I still end up with 10 cups, but it's usually more of the fruit mix and candied pineapple. The almonds used are slivered; easier to work with. The original recipe called for brandy or grape juice, but I found that substituting orange juice concentrate gives a nicer flavor.
Use 2 tube pans (10x4 each) or 5 loaf pans. Line pans with 2-3 thicknesses of brown paper or 1 thickness of heavy foil; grease well with unsalted shortening (I use lard and don't need to line with paper).
Wash raisins and currants and dry thoroughly between paper towels.
Beat butter and shortening together very thoroughly. When smooth and fluffy, add sugar gradually, beating continuously. Blend in vanilla and almond extract. Add eggs one at a time, beating after each addition. Beat in orange juice and honey. This may cause the batter to appear separated, but it will not affect results.
Sift together remaining dry ingredients. Mix prepared fruits and almonds in a large mixing bowl; add sifted dry ingredients and mix well to coat pieces. Scrape egg and butter mixture into the bowl and combine thoroughly using hands.
Fill each pan 3/4 full. Preheat oven to 275°F. Bake cakes for 1-1/2 to 3-1/2 hours, depending on size of pans used. After the first hour or so, place a pan of water on the bottom of the oven to prevent cakes from drying out. When cakes are done (to test, insert a toothpick into the center; it should come out clean), remove from oven and let pans stand on racks until cakes are completely cooled.
Remove cakes from pans while still fairly hot. Let them cool for about 15-20 minutes and then pack them while still warm. Pour 1/4 to 1/2 cup brandy (apricot, orange, or cherry) over the cakes, wrap in double thickness of foil, then wrap securely in tin foil. Store in the basement where it is cool. Every couple of days, turn the cake to ensure the brandy diffuses throughout the whole cake.
I have never had this recipe fail me. I am asked for it by most of my family members as they send them for presents at Christmas. I have tried other recipes, but this one has the moistest results I have found. By pouring the brandy on after it is cooked, you get a cake that kicks! (For our A.A. friends, I make "dry" ones with no brandy and it still stays moist.)
